Apple Scab.symptoms: Olive-green to brown velvety spots on leaves and fruit; infected leaves may twist, pucker and drop early.
Apple Scab.likely_cause: Fungus Venturia inaequalis, favoured by cool, wet spring weather.
Apple Scab.organic_treatment: Apply sulfur or lime-sulfur sprays from bud break; remove and destroy fallen leaves to cut the spore source.
Apple Scab.chemical_treatment: Protectant fungicides (captan or mancozeb) on a 7-10 day schedule during wet periods; rotate with a DMI/SDHI to limit resistance.
Apple Scab.prevention: Plant scab-resistant cultivars, prune for airflow, and rake/destroy leaf litter each autumn.

Apple with Black Rot.symptoms: Brown 'frog-eye' leaf spots, cankers on branches, and rotting fruit with concentric rings.
Apple with Black Rot.likely_cause: Fungus Botryosphaeria obtusa, entering through wounds and dead wood.
Apple with Black Rot.organic_treatment: Prune out cankered and dead wood, remove mummified fruit, and apply copper at green tip.
Apple with Black Rot.chemical_treatment: Captan or thiophanate-methyl from petal fall through summer, especially in warm wet spells.
Apple with Black Rot.prevention: Sanitise pruning tools, remove mummies and deadwood, and manage tree wounds promptly.

Cedar Apple Rust.symptoms: Bright yellow-orange spots on upper leaves; later raised tubes on the underside; fruit may be deformed.
Cedar Apple Rust.likely_cause: Fungus Gymnosporangium juniperi-virginianae needing both apple and nearby juniper/cedar hosts.
Cedar Apple Rust.organic_treatment: Remove nearby junipers/cedars where feasible and pick off galls; sulfur sprays give partial control.
Cedar Apple Rust.chemical_treatment: Myclobutanil or mancozeb from pink bud through early fruit set when spores are released.
Cedar Apple Rust.prevention: Plant rust-resistant apple varieties and avoid planting susceptible junipers nearby.

Healthy Apple.symptoms: Uniform green leaves with no lesions, spots, or distortion.
Healthy Apple.prevention: Maintain balanced nutrition and irrigation, prune for airflow, and scout weekly for early scab or rust spots.

Healthy Blueberry Plant.symptoms: Healthy green foliage with no spotting or wilting.
Healthy Blueberry Plant.prevention: Keep soil acidic (pH 4.5-5.5), mulch well, and monitor for mummy berry and leaf spots.

Cherry with Powdery Mildew.symptoms: White powdery patches on leaves and new shoots; leaves may curl and stunt.
Cherry with Powdery Mildew.likely_cause: Fungus Podosphaera clandestina, favoured by warm days and humid nights.
Cherry with Powdery Mildew.organic_treatment: Apply sulfur, potassium bicarbonate, or neem oil; prune for airflow and avoid excess nitrogen.
Cherry with Powdery Mildew.chemical_treatment: DMI or SDHI fungicides at first sign and during shoot growth, rotating modes of action.
Cherry with Powdery Mildew.prevention: Open the canopy by pruning, avoid heavy nitrogen, and choose tolerant rootstocks/varieties.

Healthy Cherry Plant.symptoms: Clean green leaves with no powdery growth or spots.
Healthy Cherry Plant.prevention: Prune for airflow, water at the base, and scout shoots for early powdery mildew.

Corn (Maize) with Cercospora and Gray Le.symptoms: Long, narrow, rectangular grey-to-tan lesions running parallel to the leaf veins.
Corn (Maize) with Cercospora and Gray Le.likely_cause: Fungus Cercospora zeae-maydis, favoured by warm humid weather and corn residue.
Corn (Maize) with Cercospora and Gray Le.organic_treatment: Rotate away from corn, bury residue by tillage, and plant resistant hybrids.
Corn (Maize) with Cercospora and Gray Le.chemical_treatment: Strobilurin or triazole fungicide at tasseling/silking if disease reaches the ear leaf early.
Corn (Maize) with Cercospora and Gray Le.prevention: Use resistant hybrids, rotate crops, and manage residue to break the disease cycle.

Corn (Maize) with Common Rust.symptoms: Small cinnamon-brown, powdery pustules scattered on both leaf surfaces.
Corn (Maize) with Common Rust.likely_cause: Fungus Puccinia sorghi, spread by airborne spores in cool, moist conditions.
Corn (Maize) with Common Rust.organic_treatment: Plant resistant hybrids; light infections rarely need treatment in field corn.
Corn (Maize) with Common Rust.chemical_treatment: Triazole/strobilurin fungicide if pustules appear early on susceptible sweet corn.
Corn (Maize) with Common Rust.prevention: Choose resistant hybrids and plant early to avoid peak spore periods.

Corn (Maize) with Northern Leaf Blight.symptoms: Long, cigar-shaped grey-green to tan lesions on the leaves.
Corn (Maize) with Northern Leaf Blight.likely_cause: Fungus Exserohilum turcicum, favoured by moderate temperatures and long leaf wetness.
Corn (Maize) with Northern Leaf Blight.organic_treatment: Rotate crops, bury infected residue, and grow resistant hybrids.
Corn (Maize) with Northern Leaf Blight.chemical_treatment: Foliar fungicide (strobilurin + triazole) from tasseling if lesions reach the ear leaf.
Corn (Maize) with Northern Leaf Blight.prevention: Resistant hybrids, crop rotation, and residue management are the main controls.

Healthy Corn (Maize) Plant.symptoms: Uniform green leaves with no lesions or pustules.
Healthy Corn (Maize) Plant.prevention: Scout the ear leaf during tasseling and maintain balanced nitrogen.

Grape with Black Rot.symptoms: Tan leaf spots with dark borders and black fruiting dots; berries shrivel into hard black mummies.
Grape with Black Rot.likely_cause: Fungus Guignardia bidwellii, overwintering in mummies and canes.
Grape with Black Rot.organic_treatment: Remove mummified berries and infected canes; apply copper or sulfur from early shoot growth.
Grape with Black Rot.chemical_treatment: Mancozeb or myclobutanil from 2-3 inch shoots through fruit set, the critical window.
Grape with Black Rot.prevention: Sanitation (remove mummies), canopy pruning for airflow, and timely early-season sprays.

Grape with Esca (Black Measles).symptoms: Tiger-stripe yellow/brown bands between leaf veins; dark spots on berries; sudden vine collapse in severe cases.
Grape with Esca (Black Measles).likely_cause: A complex of wood-rotting fungi (Phaeomoniella, Phaeoacremonium) entering through pruning wounds.
Grape with Esca (Black Measles).organic_treatment: Remove and burn affected wood; protect large pruning wounds; no cure once vines are infected.
Grape with Esca (Black Measles).chemical_treatment: No reliable curative spray; wound protectants and careful pruning hygiene are the main tools.
Grape with Esca (Black Measles).prevention: Prune in dry weather, protect/seal large wounds, and remove dead wood promptly.

Grape with Isariopsis Leaf Spot.symptoms: Irregular dark brown angular leaf spots that may merge, causing early leaf drop.
Grape with Isariopsis Leaf Spot.likely_cause: Fungus Pseudocercospora vitis (Isariopsis), favoured by warm, humid conditions.
Grape with Isariopsis Leaf Spot.organic_treatment: Improve airflow by pruning, remove fallen leaves, and apply copper-based sprays.
Grape with Isariopsis Leaf Spot.chemical_treatment: Mancozeb or other protectant fungicides during warm humid spells.
Grape with Isariopsis Leaf Spot.prevention: Canopy management for airflow and removal of leaf litter reduce inoculum.

Healthy Grape Plant.symptoms: Healthy green leaves with no spots, stripes, or mildew.
Healthy Grape Plant.prevention: Maintain an open canopy, prune in dry weather, and scout for early black rot.

Orange with Citrus Greening.symptoms: Blotchy asymmetric leaf yellowing, lopsided bitter green fruit, and twig dieback.
Orange with Citrus Greening.likely_cause: Bacterium Candidatus Liberibacter spread by the Asian citrus psyllid.
Orange with Citrus Greening.organic_treatment: Remove and destroy infected trees; control psyllids with reflective mulch and biocontrol; no cure exists.
Orange with Citrus Greening.chemical_treatment: Manage the psyllid vector with approved insecticides; there is no curative bactericide.
Orange with Citrus Greening.prevention: Use certified disease-free nursery stock, scout/control psyllids, and remove infected trees fast.

Peach with Bacterial Spot.symptoms: Small angular purple-brown leaf spots that drop out ('shot-hole'); sunken fruit lesions.
Peach with Bacterial Spot.likely_cause: Bacterium Xanthomonas arboricola pv. pruni, spread by wind-driven rain.
Peach with Bacterial Spot.organic_treatment: Apply copper sprays at low rates (watch for phytotoxicity) and improve airflow.
Peach with Bacterial Spot.chemical_treatment: Oxytetracycline or copper programs during early leaf and fruit development.
Peach with Bacterial Spot.prevention: Plant resistant varieties, avoid overhead irrigation, and shelter from wind.

Healthy Peach Plant.symptoms: Clean green foliage with no spots or shot-holes.
Healthy Peach Plant.prevention: Avoid overhead watering, prune for airflow, and scout leaves for early bacterial spots.

Bell Pepper with Bacterial Spot.symptoms: Small water-soaked leaf spots turning brown with yellow halos; raised scabby fruit lesions.
Bell Pepper with Bacterial Spot.likely_cause: Xanthomonas bacteria, spread by splashing water and infected seed.
Bell Pepper with Bacterial Spot.organic_treatment: Use copper + mancozeb, remove infected plants, and avoid working plants when wet.
Bell Pepper with Bacterial Spot.chemical_treatment: Copper-based bactericides on a preventive schedule, rotating to limit copper resistance.
Bell Pepper with Bacterial Spot.prevention: Plant certified clean seed, rotate crops, use drip irrigation, and avoid overhead watering.

Healthy Bell Pepper Plant.symptoms: Healthy green leaves and clean fruit with no spotting.
Healthy Bell Pepper Plant.prevention: Use drip irrigation, rotate crops, and scout for early water-soaked spots.

Potato with Early Blight.symptoms: Brown leaf spots with concentric 'target' rings, starting on older lower leaves.
Potato with Early Blight.likely_cause: Fungus Alternaria solani, favoured by warm weather and plant stress.
Potato with Early Blight.organic_treatment: Remove infected lower leaves, mulch, keep plants well fed, and apply copper.
Potato with Early Blight.chemical_treatment: Chlorothalonil or mancozeb on a protective schedule, starting before canopy closure.
Potato with Early Blight.prevention: Rotate crops, maintain good nutrition, avoid overhead watering, and use certified seed.

Potato with Late Blight.symptoms: Water-soaked grey-green leaf lesions turning brown/black with white mould underneath in humid weather; rapid collapse.
Potato with Late Blight.likely_cause: Oomycete Phytophthora infestans, exploding in cool, wet conditions.
Potato with Late Blight.organic_treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately; copper gives limited protection in wet spells.
Potato with Late Blight.chemical_treatment: Preventive chlorothalonil/mancozeb, switching to systemic (e.g. cymoxanil) under high pressure.
Potato with Late Blight.prevention: Plant resistant varieties, use clean seed, destroy cull piles, and act fast on first lesions.

Healthy Potato Plant.symptoms: Uniform green foliage with no spots or lesions.
Healthy Potato Plant.prevention: Scout lower leaves for early blight and watch the forecast for late-blight (cool, wet) risk.

Healthy Raspberry Plant.symptoms: Healthy green canes and leaves with no spotting or wilting.
Healthy Raspberry Plant.prevention: Thin canes for airflow, remove old fruiting canes, and scout for cane and leaf spots.

Healthy Soybean Plant.symptoms: Uniform green trifoliate leaves with no lesions.
Healthy Soybean Plant.prevention: Rotate crops, scout for rust and frogeye leaf spot, and maintain balanced fertility.

Squash with Powdery Mildew.symptoms: White powdery patches on upper and lower leaf surfaces; leaves yellow and dry out.
Squash with Powdery Mildew.likely_cause: Powdery mildew fungi (Podosphaera/Erysiphe), favoured by warm days and dry humid nights.
Squash with Powdery Mildew.organic_treatment: Spray potassium bicarbonate, sulfur, or milk solution; remove badly infected leaves.
Squash with Powdery Mildew.chemical_treatment: DMI or SDHI fungicides at first symptoms, rotating modes of action.
Squash with Powdery Mildew.prevention: Space plants for airflow, choose resistant varieties, and avoid overhead watering.

Strawberry with Leaf Scorch.symptoms: Many small dark purple spots that merge; leaf edges look scorched and dry.
Strawberry with Leaf Scorch.likely_cause: Fungus Diplocarpon earlianum, favoured by warm, wet conditions.
Strawberry with Leaf Scorch.organic_treatment: Remove infected leaves after harvest (renovation), improve airflow, and apply copper.
Strawberry with Leaf Scorch.chemical_treatment: Captan or myclobutanil during periods of active leaf growth and wet weather.
Strawberry with Leaf Scorch.prevention: Use disease-free transplants, space plants, renovate beds, and avoid overhead irrigation.

Healthy Strawberry Plant.symptoms: Clean green leaves with no spots or scorching.
Healthy Strawberry Plant.prevention: Renovate beds after harvest, keep foliage dry, and scout for leaf spots.

Tomato with Bacterial Spot.symptoms: Small water-soaked spots becoming dark and greasy; scabby raised lesions on fruit.
Tomato with Bacterial Spot.likely_cause: Xanthomonas bacteria, spread by splashing water and infected seed/transplants.
Tomato with Bacterial Spot.organic_treatment: Apply copper + mancozeb, remove infected plants, and avoid handling wet plants.
Tomato with Bacterial Spot.chemical_treatment: Copper-based bactericides preventively; rotate to manage copper resistance.
Tomato with Bacterial Spot.prevention: Use certified clean seed, rotate, use drip irrigation, and avoid overhead watering.

Tomato with Early Blight.symptoms: Brown concentric 'target' spots on older lower leaves, with yellowing around them.
Tomato with Early Blight.likely_cause: Fungus Alternaria solani/A. linariae, favoured by warm humid weather and stress.
Tomato with Early Blight.organic_treatment: Remove lower infected leaves, mulch, stake plants, and apply copper.
Tomato with Early Blight.chemical_treatment: Chlorothalonil or mancozeb on a protective schedule from early fruit set.
Tomato with Early Blight.prevention: Mulch, stake for airflow, rotate crops, and keep plants well nourished.

Tomato with Late Blight.symptoms: Large greasy grey-green blotches on leaves and stems with white mould underneath; rapid collapse.
Tomato with Late Blight.likely_cause: Oomycete Phytophthora infestans, exploding in cool, wet weather.
Tomato with Late Blight.organic_treatment: Remove and destroy infected plants immediately; copper offers limited protection.
Tomato with Late Blight.chemical_treatment: Preventive chlorothalonil/mancozeb; systemic products (cymoxanil) under high pressure.
Tomato with Late Blight.prevention: Use resistant varieties, space and stake plants, avoid leaf wetness, and act on first lesions.

Tomato with Leaf Mold.symptoms: Pale yellow spots on upper leaf surface with olive-green to brown velvety mould beneath.
Tomato with Leaf Mold.likely_cause: Fungus Passalora fulva, favoured by high humidity, common in greenhouses/polyhouses.
Tomato with Leaf Mold.organic_treatment: Improve ventilation, lower humidity, remove infected leaves, and apply copper.
Tomato with Leaf Mold.chemical_treatment: Chlorothalonil or mancozeb; ensure good coverage of the lower leaf surface.
Tomato with Leaf Mold.prevention: Ventilate, reduce humidity, space plants, and use resistant varieties under cover.

Tomato with Septoria Leaf Spot.symptoms: Many small circular spots with grey centres and dark borders on lower leaves; heavy leaf drop.
Tomato with Septoria Leaf Spot.likely_cause: Fungus Septoria lycopersici, favoured by warm wet weather and dense foliage.
Tomato with Septoria Leaf Spot.organic_treatment: Remove infected lower leaves, mulch to stop soil splash, and apply copper.
Tomato with Septoria Leaf Spot.chemical_treatment: Chlorothalonil or mancozeb every 7-10 days during wet weather.
Tomato with Septoria Leaf Spot.prevention: Mulch, stake plants, rotate crops, and avoid overhead irrigation.

Tomato with Spider Mites or Two-spotted.symptoms: Fine yellow stippling/bronzing of leaves with delicate webbing; leaves dry and drop in heat.
Tomato with Spider Mites or Two-spotted.likely_cause: Two-spotted spider mite (Tetranychus urticae), thriving in hot, dry, dusty conditions.
Tomato with Spider Mites or Two-spotted.organic_treatment: Spray water/insecticidal soap or neem oil on leaf undersides; release predatory mites.
Tomato with Spider Mites or Two-spotted.chemical_treatment: A targeted miticide (not a broad insecticide, which can flare mites); rotate chemistries.
Tomato with Spider Mites or Two-spotted.prevention: Keep plants watered and dust-free, avoid broad-spectrum insecticides, and scout leaf undersides.

Tomato with Target Spot.symptoms: Brown spots with concentric rings on leaves and sunken circular lesions on fruit.
Tomato with Target Spot.likely_cause: Fungus Corynespora cassiicola, favoured by warm, humid conditions.
Tomato with Target Spot.organic_treatment: Remove infected leaves, improve airflow, and apply copper.
Tomato with Target Spot.chemical_treatment: Chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or an SDHI/strobilurin during humid spells.
Tomato with Target Spot.prevention: Stake for airflow, rotate crops, and avoid prolonged leaf wetness.

Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus.symptoms: Upward leaf curling, yellow leaf margins, stunting, and heavy flower/fruit drop.
Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus.likely_cause: Begomovirus (TYLCV) transmitted by the whitefly Bemisia tabaci.
Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus.organic_treatment: Remove infected plants, use reflective mulch and yellow sticky traps, and protect with insect netting.
Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus.chemical_treatment: Manage whitefly vectors with approved insecticides; there is no cure for the virus itself.
Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus.prevention: Plant resistant varieties, use insect-proof nurseries/netting, and control whiteflies early.

Tomato Mosaic Virus.symptoms: Mottled light/dark green mosaic, leaf distortion, fern-like narrow leaves, and reduced yield.
Tomato Mosaic Virus.likely_cause: Tomato mosaic virus (ToMV), spread mechanically by hands, tools, and infected seed.
Tomato Mosaic Virus.organic_treatment: Remove infected plants, disinfect hands/tools (milk or bleach), and avoid tobacco handling near plants.
Tomato Mosaic Virus.chemical_treatment: No chemical cures viruses; focus on sanitation and vector/contact control.
Tomato Mosaic Virus.prevention: Use resistant varieties and clean seed, wash hands/tools, and rogue out infected plants.

Healthy Tomato Plant.symptoms: Uniform green leaves with no spots, curling, or mosaic.
Healthy Tomato Plant.prevention: Mulch, stake for airflow, water at the base, and scout weekly for early blight and whiteflies.
